I think its the 10 minutes of light therapy before bed, with its subtraction of yellow. When I'm out at first light the sky lightens, but the slow crescendo of yellow appears before the orange and red. When it reaches a certain level, the birds start chirping, even before sunrise. I like to think the birds help call the sunrise in.  I was out at 4 a.m. this morning, lying on the ground looking at the moon and the stars. I've started keeping as warm as I need to, not trying to make it an air CT time, I stay out longer. Just make sure I'm grounded underneath, and then cosy up under the blankets. But I was looking first at the moon, (the light reflected being more in the now ....) and then at the stars. I was thinking of my eyes emitting photons which were interacting with photons from stars whose light had left them thousands of years ago. Had I found the photons or did they find me? (I've thought about that in the past, but this morning I was re-mind-ed). I was thinking about a woman's years connected to her moon time, versus a post menopausal woman now has time to look beyond her moon time to the stars. If she is fully connected during the day, is the naturally lowering melatonin levels of aging her chance to experience more of the quantum All That Is during grounded, colder, darkness wakefulness without the penalty of sunlight speeding up time? Came back in to find Reality 8, how women gain time at menopause..... Gotta just love a bit of synchronicity.

beautifully said Sue........

I am thinking that adding in hormones post menopause is speeding up time ......shouldn't we learn to "just be"?

Shouldn't we just let Mother Nature do her "thang"???

I am way calmer these days, way more vulnerable, way more open to just "being", way more open to what life has to offer.

At some moment in Time ....I stepped off the tread mill and really noticed my world and all the simple and wonderful treasures of my life....

### caroline — 2017-03-27T11:00:19+0300

### Mystic Rose60 — 2017-03-27T17:22:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/reality-8.19284/post-211222>

What a great thread and thanks for starting it Caroline . The past two years have been a journey of healing and self discovery for me. A beautiful and at times, heart wrenching time of self reflection and growth here. Something profound happened when I let go and I let Nature and I haven't looked back since. As a result of letting go and letting Nature, I feel more alive and vibrant than I have in years, perhaps decades. I haven't gone back to look at my journal, but from what I can remember writing, I'm no longer that same woman. Gratitude! Perhaps this is why I become emotional when I see others who are having such great success here when they just relax and allow to let Nature heal.

Every morning, when I awaken, the very first thing that I do, is to give gratitude and I actually choose a word for the day and I write my feelings, in my gratitude journal, based on this one word. Today's was was "afraid." I also enjoy listening to TED Talks and after I journaled, went online to see what TED Talks I could find based on this one word. This is the talk that I found this morning and I feel that it's a great talk to share with you two gals and anyone else who wants to listen. Scilla touched my heart this morning. Enjoy!

### Sue-UK — 2017-03-28T09:59:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/reality-8.19284/post-211263>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=211255)
>
> what a great thought to ponder Sue .... I think you are on to something!

My n=1 ponderings

I know men can and do raise children, but in terms of menopause …..Bringing up my children, I think I was happy to accept the benefits of society’s norms , the (fertility?) box, as part of my kid’s environment, IF my kids benefited from it. The problems arise when they don’t, or if a crisis occurs, or if the box worked for one but not another. That forced me to look outside the box more than once. Society’s norms now include less time outside, nnEMF, wifi, etc etc, so my granddaughters are facing an environment that my children weren’t facing at the same age. Although some may choose not to, (or may not have children at all), a woman may also put her own dreams on hold while she rears the next generation, or takes responsibility for ensuring the wellbeing of her or her partner’s parents until they pass. She may know that the possibility of there not being a box exists, and even experience it as moments of grace prior to menopause, if her redox is high enough.

I think there’s a truth in the saying that a woman is only as happy as her saddest child, women willingly give up their energy to their children or loved ones, even if it leaves them on their redox knees. If consciousness emerges from mitochondria, then how we experience menopause may depend on whether we can meet it head on from a position of redox strength, or whether we reach it crawling on all fours. But even if a woman makes it crawling to menopause, historically we are the survivors of famine, war and pestilence. Do the losses of males in battle make survival of the human species dependent on female longevity? Our experience also has survival value to the next generation.

With the right knowledge and the ability to apply it, I think out of the box or no box thinking is the opportunity to get back on her feet. If a woman understands the issues and makes an informed choice about temporarily supplementing with exogenous hormones, its one thing, but in a low redox state there is a higher risk is not understanding it fully from a quantum perspective and sleepwalking into it, often on the "knowledgeable advice" of some random person on the internet. Reality 8 isn't an easy read for a newbie, but what I love about it is that it isn't just about knowledge and science, its about wisdom ... it speaks to that part of my being that recognises it as such, I am re-mind-ed. Dopamine lift from that thought! I want my daughter and granddaughters to know this stuff ..... How better to show them than by walking the walk .. Mirror menopause to them as a gift, not a curse ...

So I'm not sure where this is leading me, but I'll take the first step anyway ....
I was thinking about ferritin and iron's role in menopause .... and then wondered what happened if a woman was low on ferritin and iron when she entered the menopause transition. Is it possible for there to be no symptoms (other than her periods stopping), so she thinks she is sailing through it. But she is losing energy from the "wave part" of sunlight, but can't access the particle aspect of light in the same way, to harvest more magnetic energy from the sun's rays naturally to gain more energy back than she lost in the process of menopause. She loses, but doesn't gain. Does time march on as before, or does it -more likely? - speed up. Or if redox is bad, does keeping ferritin and iron low stop her from frying her mitochondria? Waiting for a better environment?

I can see how the absence of menopausal symptoms could be a good thing (losing wave, gaining particle, good redox) or a bad thing (losing wave, no particle gain, poorer redox). Need to think about this a bit more in relation to my own menopause transition ...

sounds like ordinary life right? but i suppose it was/is my perception of these moments in time that makes them feel like lifetimes - there was a beginning a middle and an end (apparently) - then another opportunity - to think and be different or access different qualities in my own evolving self - still the same person yet at the same time not - anyway - this paragraph i just wrote seemed like it took a lifetime <g> jk

I know Dr kruse talks of the importance of Morning Sun ,which I do on a regular basis here in Sydney Australia,
My question is are there still benefits with receiving afternoon Sunlight?

### Sue-UK — 2017-03-27T10:08:11+0300

> [Brent Patrick said:](/goto/post?id=211190)

I think definitely yes. With afternoon sun I am still harvesting photons, EZ battery charging etc, and my brain is registering the changing circadian rhythm of the decrescendoing frequencies heading for sunset.

### Brent Patrick — 2017-03-27T10:14:50+0300

### Sue-UK — 2017-03-27T10:55:04+0300

> Thank you Sue for your input. I was thinking the same regarding the cycle of sunlight throughout the Day, I work in a Maximum Security Prison and see first hand the result of Inmate mental degradation due to lack of sunlight (yard time).

Interesting. I was once paid by a charity to teach a prison volunteer working with lifers or those with long sentences how to run a meditation group using visualisation of the denied light (and colour, sounds, etc). The idea was that the volunteer introduced the different possibilities to the group, and from the experiences the prisoner could pick which one(s) suited them best, and use them on their own to help manage their own stress (downstream benefits for the staff?). Shame I didn't get any feedback on how it went, but it was very thought provoking setting up the programme for the volunteer.

### Brent Patrick — 2017-04-05T09:57:45+0300

especially non native EMF you are quick to see the self destruction we humans are doing to ourselves. Gaol is the perfect environment too see this.

As Jack mentioned drug use is proportional to electromagnetic sensitivity is a perfect example,,, an inmate comes in with drug/alcohol issues usually goes through a detox period and what do we do
during this process we place him/her in a camera cell with painted concrete floor (no magnetism) and 24hr fluorescent lights for observation and feed him /her a high Carb diet all year round (No seafood).
The inmate usually comes out with the life sucked out of them mood disorders, lack of energy and not able to sleep with circadian rhythms shot to pieces. I SHAKE MY HEAD!

Yes Inger your idea Gaol would be a great way too help Inmates build there dopamine levels to make better life choices and hopefully reduce recidivism and be better members of community

I try and motivate Inmates to read more but they have such low Dopamine levels and are full of medication the motivation and drive is not there.

But having said this inmates who go to prison farms fare much better better food (usually grown by inmates) and plenty of morning sun and grounding (instinctively Inmates will takes there shoes off to play football on the grass)

The difference between prison farm inmates and city prison Inmates is like chalk and cheese

By observing the above and learning Jacks work has really helped me connect the dots,,it is very fascinating to say the least and is a work in progress

### Rubicon — 2017-04-06T00:37:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flicker-effect.19286/post-211843>

This LinkedIn post reminded me of something I saw on flicker a few weeks ago.

Creator of Iris, Daniel Georgiev, on LCD/LED monitor brightness and why your monitor flickers: [http://iristech.co/brightness/](http://iristech.co/brightness/)

**Summary**
Monitors that use Pulse Width Modulation (PWM) to reduce brightness introduce breaks in the display where the LED's turn off for a very short time. Those breaks create imperceptible flicker
Another method to dim the monitor called DC Dimming reduces brightness without causing flicker
It's possible to use DC dimming on your monitor with the right software on your computer and avoid flicker
Iris uses DC dimming

**How to eliminate flicker on any monitor**

1.  Using the buttons on the front of your monitor, adjust the brightness to maximum
2.  Use software like Iris to reduce the brightness to an acceptable level, as low as you'd like

That's it, your brightness is adjusted without introducing flicker.

Another option is to buy a monitor that does not use PWM to dim the screen so you don't get flicker. Daniel has a list of some monitors that might work on the Iris site: [http://iristech.co/flicker-free-monitors/](http://iristech.co/flicker-free-monitors/)

Iris does not help with flicker if your monitor flickers at 100% brightness. There is a lot of monitors that flicker even when the brightness is at 100% Every monitor is different some don't start flickering till you dim them below a certain value. I bought the full version of Iris and tested this with two monitors that flickered at all levels of brightness and it doesn't help. Sometimes even the monitors that say flicker free aren't even flicker free. Alexander Wunsch recommends testing them before you buy them if you have a way to do it. I am using a BenQ flicker free and I can tell you that this is a dud because it is not flicker free. I have seen people complaining about buying monitors listed as flicker free and they aren't. I think Iris is good for controlling color temperature though.

### Jason36 — 2017-05-06T17:56:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flicker-effect.19286/post-213506>

> [Rubicon said:](/goto/post?id=213488)
>
> Thanks for the info @[Jason36](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/19820/)
>
> In your testing, for a monitor that is flicker free were you able to confirm that Iris will lower the brightness and change the color temperature without introducing flicker?
>
> What method did you use to test flicker?
>
> I would like to test my own monitors.

I guess you could say my test isn't accurate. I am highly sensitive to flicker to the point where I can't even use a monitor or any monitor for that matter. I have struggled for 9 years using a computer with mostly having my computers hooked up to a Plasma TV because CCFL and LED kill me. I can barely look at a flickering screen for more then a few minutes without dying of eye pain. I can even feel the pain if a monitor is in my general vision but I am not looking at it. I have talked with a few people on the Internet with the exact same problem as me and I feel that a lot of people don't understand this issue correctly. I also understand it's hard to know about it when you can't feel the flicker. I have tested two CCFL monitors with IRIS plus one LED monitor that is supposed to be flicker free( I know the BENQ monitor I own is a dud because I have the same model at work and it is flicker free) and none of them worked. I even emailed the IRIS guy and he told me it doesn't work with all monitors. The reason it doesn't work is because lots of monitors use PWM even at 100% brightness. His program he is saying turn the brightness of your monitor to 100% then use the software to turn it down. That will not help if your monitor is flickering at 100% brightness. Alexander Wunsch says using an old cell phone camera is a simple way to test for flicker. Aim the camera at the screen and film it and you will see the flicker. Most new phones have a filter on them so you can't see the flicker anymore. I don't need to do this because I can feel the flicker immediately when I sit in front of a terrible monitor. My recommendation is just buy a flicker free monitor to begin with. I can confirm at work I have used Iris on my monitor at work and it doesn't flicker when I use the software to lower the brightness. If you buy a flicker free monitor you can just dim the brightness without it flickering anyways. I would use IRIS to turn the color temperature down to 3000 at night though not to disrupt your day/night cycles.

I believe more information needs to be done on screen usage and what is the best screen you can use. Hardly any of us can avoid it with our jobs and the poor kids in school these days. I am of the opinion that flicker free is not good enough. All these monitor companies saying their monitors our eye safe and good for the eyes because they don't flicker is ridiculous. All LED monitors our mostly the same in that the spectrum is garbage and it's all blue and no red. If you will pull up a typical spectrum of a Plasma TV you will see a giant spike in red and green. That is why I have never had any issues with these TV's. LED can't produce red or green. I believe that Samsung may have solved that issue with their quantum dot monitors. If you look at their spectrum chart it is almost identical to a plasma TV. Not to mention their quantum dot monitors our flicker free. The red color cancels out blue. OLED is also probably next solution if you can find it in flicker free. If you pull a spectrum chart of OLED you will see a little lower blue and a higher green and red. Alexander Wunsch also mentioned that when using OLED when the device is displaying black you are getting true black and not a closed shutter blocking light which is used in normal LED. Then you are not being directly exposed to radiation of the LED.

This is the color spectrum of a typical Samsung quantum dot TV. Now look at the color spectrum of a Plasma TV.

I believe the ultimate solution would be for a quantum dot monitor to be made with a 3000 color temperature instead of the typical 6500 that is used. I forget the name of the company but someone at made an E-Ink computer monitor. That would be the ultimate for most of your computer needs and just turn on a second display when you need color. I think the E-Ink was going for around 1300 dollars which is way too much money. My daughter is very educated when it comes different types of artificial light and I always hear about all the nonsense going on in the school. They make the kids all use these cheap netbooks with terrible flicker and high blue light. I bought her clear blue blocking glasses that filter up to 460NM the same ones I wear. She even showed the class when she put the lens of her glasses in the LED light of the projector how it deflects a bunch of the blue. She even tells me she notices when she goes to different peoples houses who use CFL bulbs and LED TV's how much harsher it is then our plasma TV's and Incandescent's. I own 3 plasma TV's because I despise LED

### Rubicon — 2017-05-06T20:07:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flicker-effect.19286/post-213517>

Thanks Jason! Awesome info.

Sorry to hear that you're a "flicker canary". I have an old friend that I believe likely has the same issue you do. A few years ago when LED monitors first became available he couldn't use them due to visual issues and was quite distressed. At the time I had no idea what he was talking about, but I think I'll try to contact him and confirm if he's got a visual flicker issue.

I did some flicker testing today and found:

\- a short video that shows what flicker looks like through an old phone: [Measuring Light Bulb Flicker with Nothing More than a Cell Phone](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Qym5-126BDY)
\- using the camera on an iPhone 4 I could see what looks like the flicker pattern on my lights, and most of my monitors
\- the iPhone app [Flicker Tester](http://www.visosystems.com/products/flicker-tester/) (worked to test an incandescent flood light on a dimmer)
\- the android app [Oulight](https://play.google.com/store/apps/details?id=fi.aunio.oulight&hl=en) (Looks good but a paid app, not compatible with my devices, didn't try)

When measuring flicker with Viso from an incandescent flood light on a dimmer, it registered more flicker at the highest power setting than at the lowest:

I have been collaborating on with [Jason Bawden-Smith](https://www.facebook.com/jason.bawdensmith) and many others to create [EMFWarriors.com.](http://EMFWarriors.com/) Within a week, the entire world will have free access to the EMFWarrior's library: gearing up to be the largest EMF resource and database of information on the planet ever assembled with continual expansion into the future. It's going to be epic (I'm biased), but yeah, epic with some uber amazingness! More to come very soon. Did I say it was free? Yep. EMFWarriors.com is currently active if you want to sign up and create a free account, but won't give access until the library goes live next week.

If any of you have powerful EMF links, articles, blogs, etc. that you believe I may not have, I'm your guy and I have been vetting and organizing the material going into the library as a curator. I have A LOT up there now, but since it's a real library, and our EMF world is forever changing out from under us, the library will continue to grow over time. As you would expect, there are many links to JK's resources here and what is available to the public on Youtube and LinkedIn.

# Thread: Body composition tests. Which is best?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/body-composition-tests-which-is-best.19291/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 4

### CFAN — 2017-03-29T19:45:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/body-composition-tests-which-is-best.19291/post-211339>

Hi,
In the book, it mentions a DEXA scan. My doctor wouldn't order it since he believes it is only for osteoporosis. So I have to go outside him. I have found via another site's blog about private testing that a local medical university would be a good way to get it, though I would have to pay out of pocket. Luckily I live near Ohio State, so I can go to their world famous med school for it.

However, when I went to their site for pricing. They had some other options that intrigued me. I do not know their benefit vs JUST an DEXA scan. Whether I should try for all of them, or just some.

Can anyone advise? The other options are: skinfolds, hydrostatic weighing, and BOD POD

[http://eslab.ehe.osu.edu/body-composition/](http://eslab.ehe.osu.edu/body-composition/)

### Jack Kruse — 2017-03-30T00:15:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/body-composition-tests-which-is-best.19291/post-211348>

One that uses X-ray.........but I only like it once every 3 years because of it.

### JanSz — 2017-03-30T16:02:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/body-composition-tests-which-is-best.19291/post-211394>

You have already lost one inch of height.
At 29yo, you already know that you have problem in that area.

..

# Thread: Questions from the mind of a 7 year old.....
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/questions-from-the-mind-of-a-7-year-old.19293/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 31
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Stardust — 2017-03-30T01:39:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/questions-from-the-mind-of-a-7-year-old.19293/post-211355>

I try to explain the things I do and learn to my children. I have a 5-year-old and a 7-year-old. I was explaining to my 7 -year-old what cold thermogenesis is and why I will be doing it. She asked me if she could try it. I did not have an answer for her. I told her I would post here and ask. Is it safe for a 7-year-old to cold adapt? If so, would we use the cold thermo protocol?

### Jack Kruse — 2017-03-30T02:27:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/questions-from-the-mind-of-a-7-year-old.19293/post-211357>

She can. We have many kids who do better with it than their parents.

### drezy — 2017-03-30T02:32:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/questions-from-the-mind-of-a-7-year-old.19293/post-211359>

Given that the point of much of this is that us mammals can become cold adapted again, I went ahead and let my 12 yo son do it once a week. If we were in cold caves way back then our kids were too, right?

No problems 4 weeks in.

My boy is large for his size and is really broad shouldered. If he were smaller and really lean I'd decrease his time and/or temperature,but his latest CT was 56˚ for 40 min

### Jack Kruse — 2017-03-30T02:33:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/questions-from-the-mind-of-a-7-year-old.19293/post-211361>

Every kid.......wants to go out with no clothes on or hardly any clothes when they young and its snowing. What and who stops them? Why? Conventional belief. Think about that. Does the other mother mammals on this planet do as the human primate mother with their young with respect to temperature?

### drezy — 2017-03-30T02:49:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/questions-from-the-mind-of-a-7-year-old.19293/post-211364>

Just won't look as cool without, The North Face, Patagonia, Arc'Teryx, or Marmot.

### Mystic Rose60 — 2017-03-30T17:00:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/questions-from-the-mind-of-a-7-year-old.19293/post-211399>

Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

These are my two grandsons, and they are both cold adapted little guys. Like their Grandma, they love the cold. They know the routine of CT and warm up by the IR fire before heading outdoors to run around and boy do they laugh! They are 26 and 13 months in these photos that were taken in January of this year. It was 27 degrees with a slight windchill and the snow was just starting to fall and dust the ground. They also take cold dips in lake Michigan. Other than each having just a few slight sniffles that disappeared within a day or so, they have not been sick. Nature is their drug and they have thrived as a result of it.

### Mystic Rose60 — 2017-03-30T17:46:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/questions-from-the-mind-of-a-7-year-old.19293/post-211405>

It's been 9 or so years since a baby butt graced my house. I always smile and chuckle when a baby butt goes strolling by. It's a reminder to me that all these rules and concerns in my head are mostly of my own making. Baby butts don't care. Baby butts are like the honey badger.

### Dia — 2017-03-31T17:54:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/questions-from-the-mind-of-a-7-year-old.19293/post-211473>

As a little girl in JA, I remembered my grandmother doing some strange things that only now are making sense to me. One thing that stood out for me... I had a cousin that at three years old was still not walking. Every morning my grandmother would hold him down in a tub of cold water up to his waist, for about ten minutes or so, then she would put him waist deep into a hole that she had dug, and kept him buried there for about four, five hours. Fed him from the hole and everything. He would cry to exhaustion, then fell asleep standing up, while buried in this hole. After about 4 weeks of this torture, he started walking! It was crazy!

I think she would have been charge for child abuse if she had lived in North America, and did that.

Every morning my grandmother would hold him down in a tub of cold water up to his waist, for about ten minutes or so, then she would put him waist deep into a hole that she had dug, and kept him buried there for about four, five hours. Fed him from the hole and everything. He would cry to exhaustion, then fell asleep standing up, while buried in this hole. After about 4 weeks of this torture, he started walking! It was crazy!
>
> I think she would have been charge for child abuse if she had lived in North America, and did that.

### JanSz — 2017-03-31T22:17:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/questions-from-the-mind-of-a-7-year-old.19293/post-211487>

> [Dia said:](/goto/post?id=211485)
>
> Yea, you know what was strange as well... even though we lived in the equatorial region. Every single morning my grandmother would pour cold water on our heads for a few second, as soon as we wake up.

When one stays in one place for at least few generations, old folks wisdom is generated.
Your grandma learned her wisdom somewhere, locally.
Poland is not settled place for the last few centuries.
There are families that have multi generational roots, not that many.
My family was uprooted number of times and number of ways.
Grandfather spend his youth in New York State being there always temporarily so he could feed his wife and children that lived in Poland. He was ready to retire, then WWII happened. On my mother's site was even worst.
Things like that adds value to the family I was able to create.

..

### drezy — 2017-03-31T22:31:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/questions-from-the-mind-of-a-7-year-old.19293/post-211488>

> [Stardust said:](/goto/post?id=211355)
>
> I try to explain the things I do and learn to my children. I have a 5-year-old and a 7-year-old. I was explaining to my 7 -year-old what cold thermogenesis is and why I will be doing it. She asked me if she could try it. I did not have an answer for her. I told her I would post here and ask. Is it safe for a 7-year-old to cold adapt? If so, would we use the cold thermo protocol?

@[Stardust](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/19767/)

You made me really think about your kid's approach. It's kind of a "What is it and and when can I try it?" approach.

Adults sometimes need 1 television doctor, 2 surgeon generals, and a few industrialized nations to acknowledge something before we maybe start considering it. This is often 40+years(half a generation) after first proof of a concept. Although I don't undergo massive regular testing I've come to understand my main issues and "check engine lights" in between testing. Not to pimp my own post but I place a high value in understanding how you personally breakdown and asked in this forum if others do [https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.p...r-bodys-check-engine-light.19241/#post-210689](https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/what-is-your-bodys-check-engine-light.19241/#post-210689) . If you have a good sense of these immediate feedback factors, you'll know in short order if anything you try here has basic benefit in between any tests.

You and I were taught science but your 7 year old is basically saying "Where's the data and let's see if we can replicate it." There's some natural/purer science! Encourage curiosity. TedEd has some interesting info. Even just today Jack mentioned Lynn Marguiles. Here is a kid friendly-ish video that kids might like on cell evolution that is a baby on-ramp for a portion what is unfolding here:

Kids might not get all the science, but they learn and absorb way faster than we even give them credit for.

# Thread: Hello From Canada!!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-canada.19294/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jason36 — 2017-03-30T03:35:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-canada.19294/post-211368>

I am so excited to have discovered Jack Kruse and the amazing knowledge he has regarding blue light and nnEMF and the damage it causes. I have been all over the Internet for 9 years looking for answers to my issues and finally I found someone besides Alexander Wunsch who is talking about how bad LED and CFL lights are for your health.

My 3 issues that I am trying to cure are flicker/blue light sensitivity and IBS. I think the flicker issue is my worst I am so frustrated with it. I have been trying for 9 years to cure it with expensive organic useless food that has done nothing except drain my bank account. When I discovered Alexander Wunsch I started going outside a lot through out the whole winter. Then about 7 weeks ago I discovered Jack Kruse on YouTube and I watched probably 30 of his videos while taking notes. I read Jack's book and am currently reading a book by Nick Lane called the Vital Question. Next I am going to read Oxygen by Nick Lane. I have half read the 4th phase of water by Gerald Pollack. I am going to read a bunch more books that Jack has listed in his podcasts and blogs.

So after the first night of watching videos from Jack I immediately went to findaspring.com and found a spring and have never missed a day of drinking only spring water. The interesting thing about where I live in Canada a bunch of the cities around here don't use fluoride in their water. I do understand why spring water is better though and will never go back to tap water. One of the things I guess I have always been doing right is eating fish. I almost ate sardines every day for the last 6 years. I also liked Sockeye Salmon a lot. I have increased my quantities on fish and am eating oysters a lot now. I'm guessing I didn't eat enough fish because I still have my flickering sensitivity to lights

I used to ground a lot and meditate outside but then the food gurus would have me convinced that food was the cause of my health issues. So I would waste my time and focus on food again and stop doing what was important to make myself better like get the heck outside! and CT! I have spent the last 7 weeks since I discovered Jack being outside in the cold as much as possible. Some weekends I would spend the entire day outside. I can tell you today was the first day UV was back because I could feel it in my eyes. It may have started a few days earlier but we have had quite a few cloudy days. This was a good thing because I feel great.

I have never had an issue going outside in the sun without sun glasses because I have not been wearing them for 5 years already. I figured this out on my own and never believed anyone who told me differently because I always used to say to my wife that I only feel good in natural light. I always feel terrible in fake light. I use only clear Incandescent light blubs in my house. I still haven't found the DC 12V lamp that Alexander Wunsch has recommended. I have used Plasma TV's for 5 years because of the giant spike of red light in them and because I can't tolerate anything related to LED and CCFL.

I have barely ever owned a cell phone because I can't use them because of the terrible screens and flicker. I used to own a Samsung S2 with an OLED screen that didn't flicker but couldn't tolerate it anymore when they changed all my lights at my work to LED. At the very least they got flicker free LED but even with the SCT orange goggles I find them hard to deal with still because blue light can leak in a few spots. I recently just got a Yotaphone 2 because it has an E-Ink screen. It barely ever gets used though and is never on my body. The wifi goes off every night in my house and everything is wired up. I want to get rid of the wireless router. It's just my wife who likes to use her cell phone on our wifi that is preventing that.

After all this studying with Jack I can pretty much pin point where everything went wrong in my life in regards to my health. It was back in 2008 when I was 28 years old I lived on the 17th floor of an apartment working night shift and had just bought a laptop and you guessed it would set it on my lap to use it. I don't think at that time I had any CFL(swirly bulbs I can't stand them!!!) lights in my house but my work was terrible with the fluorescent tubes. They had just switched them over to the newer fluorescent lights that flicker way more and that was the end of me. I also used to own a portable gaming system that had a CFL light and of course used PWM and that ended quickly because I had to stop using it.

The frustrating part was at that time nobody on the Internet had much to say about this issue. No doctor has ever helped me with this issue. I have pretty much just tolerated the pain of flickering for so many years. The only luck I had was finding forums of people with the same problem but nobody really having a solution to it. I am so happy to have found Jack and finally feel like someone knows what the heck is going on. I believe I need to recycle my Mitocondria so that they have an aquapora 4 gate. That is my goal. Jack thanks so much for providing all of us with this great information. You are changing the world!!!

# Thread: Cosmosapiens
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cosmosapiens.19300/>
**Subforum:** Factor X
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### nonchalant — 2017-03-31T19:16:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cosmosapiens.19300/post-211474>

Nervous systems
On page 399 of John Hind's book, he says flatworms were among the first invertebrates to exhibit bilateral symmetry (they have two parallel nerve cords running the length of the body, with eyespots at one end.) It is easy for us here to see that the eyespots would provide energy down both nerve cords. Hind says that the question of why bilateral symmetry is important hasn't been satisfactorily answered. But looking at the picture on the next page, wouldn't electricity traveling down the parallel cords make a nice magnetic induction field inside to benefit the worm?

And looking at the picture of the hydra, the nerve network surrounding the tubular body would create a magnetic induction coil. When it is firmly attached to the earth, it has plenty of energy for that mouth at the top and all the tentacles. Aren't tube-shaped organisms some of the first fossils, whether they had a nerve network or a bilateral system?

Speaking of eyespots... Wouldn't the eyespots create more energy for the invertebrate, to allow it to move more often, or even swim freely? Which came first, the eye, or the ability to swim (rather than drift)?

### Jack Kruse — 2017-03-31T20:12:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cosmosapiens.19300/post-211475>

Hind says that the question of why bilateral symmetry is important hasn't been satisfactorily answered. But looking at the picture on the next page, wouldn't electricity traveling down the parallel cords make a nice magnetic induction field inside to benefit the worm? yes........but what increases the effect? A twist.......that is why dna twists and why the neural tubes of all higher animals twists into the neocortex..........= sulci and grooves

### Jack Kruse — 2017-04-01T05:31:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kinetic-alfven-waves-link-to-plasma-creation-in-mitochondria-h.19303/post-211510>

What is HAARP?

The High-frequency Active Auroral Research Program, or HAARP, is a scientific endeavor aimed at studying the properties and behavior of the ionosphere. Operation of the research facility was transferred from the United States Air Force to the University of Alaska Fairbanks on Aug. 11, 2015, allowing HAARP to continue with exploration of ionospheric phenomenology via a land-use cooperative research and development agreement.

HAARP is the world's most capable high-power, high-frequency transmitter for study of the ionosphere. The HAARP program is committed to developing a world-class ionospheric research facility consisting of:

The Ionospheric Research Instrument, a high power transmitter facility operating in the High Frequency range. The IRI can be used to temporarily excite a limited area of the ionosphere for scientific study.
A sophisticated suite of scientific or diagnostic instruments that can be used to observe the physical processes that occur in the excited region.

Observation of the processes resulting from the use of the IRI in a controlled manner will allow scientists to better understand processes that occur continuously under the natural stimulation of the sun.

Scientific instruments installed at the HAARP Observatory can also be used for a variety of continuing research efforts which do not involve the use of the IRI but are strictly passive. These include ionospheric characterization using satellite beacons, telescopic observation of the fine structure in the aurora and documentation of long-term variations in the ozone layer.

I hope all of you realize that sunlight is designed to hit the ozone layer to eliminate UV light below 250nm from getting to Earth and the collision of the UV light below 250 nm makes oxygen in our atomosphere. "Light-responsive agents offer the promise of targeted therapy, whose benefits include (i) prolonged action at the target site, (ii) overall reduced systemic dosage, (iii) reduced adverse effects, and (iv) localized delivery of multiple agents. Although photoactivated prodrugs have been reported, these species generally require short wavelengths (<450 nm) for activation. However, maximal tissue penetrance by light occurs within the “optical window of tissue” (600–900 nm), well beyond the wavelength range of most existing photocleavable functional groups. Furthermore, since multidrug therapy holds promise for the treatment of complex diseases, from cancer to neurological disorders, controlling the action of multiple drugs via wavelength modulation would take advantage of a property that is unique to light. However, discrimination between existing photoresponsive moieties has thus far proven to be limited." ### Jack Kruse — 2017-04-01T05:37:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kinetic-alfven-waves-link-to-plasma-creation-in-mitochondria-h.19303/post-211513>

Protein kinase C is a phosphorus (March 2017 webinar) containing enzyme that takes light and charge separates water into hydrogen and electricity. Zinc acts like a molecular wire to release neurotransmitters. A mitochondria is hydrogen fuel cell converts chemical energy stored by hydrogen fuel into electricity. Protein kinase C and Zn work with mitochondria to get that done in neurons. Hydrogen fuel cells and batteries are both electrochemical cells. They each have two electrodes in contact with a material that can conduct ions, called an electrolyte. One electrode is the anode and the other is the cathode. **In a hydrogen fuel cell electrons are released from the hydrogen that is supplied to the anode whereas in a battery the electrons are released from the material in the anode itself.**

Because battery electrodes actively participate in the conversion of chemical energy to electrical energy, over time this can have a damaging effect on the electrodes and therefore on the effectiveness of the battery. Unlike batteries, the electrodes in hydrogen fuel cells are relatively stable since they act as catalysts in the release or acceptance of electrons and are not chemically changed during this process.
When pure hydrogen is used as the fuel, the only by-products generated from the fuel cell are pure water and heat. This makes fuel cells potentially very efficient devices with minimal environmental impact. Often both of these by-products can be put to some kind of use by the mitochondria in our cells by making the DC electric current or plasma of life. Human UBIAD1 localizes to mitochondria and converts vitamin K(1) to vitamin K(2). Vitamin K(2) is best known as a cofactor in blood coagulation, but in bacteria it is a membrane-bound electron carrier. Whether vitamin K(2) exerts a similar carrier function in eukaryotic cells is unknown. In the literature UBIAD1/Heix as a modifier of pink1, a gene mutated in Parkinson's disease that affects mitochondrial function to lead to high heteroplasmy rates. Researchers found that vitamin K(2) was necessary and sufficient to transfer electrons in mitochondrial models. Heix mutants showed severe mitochondrial defects that were rescued by vitamin K(2), and, similar to ubiquinone (CoEnQ10), vitamin K(2) transferred electrons in these mitochondria, resulting in more efficient adenosine triphosphate (ATP) production. Thus, mitochondrial dysfunction was rescued by vitamin K(2) that serves as a mitochondrial electron carrier, helping to maintain normal ATP production. If we have a mitochondria that uses H+ hydrogen plasma as a fuel cell does that mean that maybe the sun is a electric star?

Because hydrogen gas does not occur naturally in the environment of Earth, hydrogen fuel must be derived from other substances that contain hydrogen such as methanol, gasoline, natural gas, and **water**. Nature chose water for cells, but man chose fossil fuels to get its hydorgen. Most hydrogen that is produced today comes from natural gas on Earth in manufactured hydrogen fuel cells.

If hydrogen is made from water the only byproduct is pure water. Now stop and think about what I told you a mitochondria makes.........CO2 and water.

When heteroplasmy rate is high human mitochondria produce more CO2 and less water because they are burning more carbon sources from food and not using H+ in the mitochondrial matrix. As a result these people will have more CO2 in their blood and gut. They will tend to burp more and fart more. They also will be dehydrated chronically because they cannot generate enough water from beta oxidation of fats. The more glucose they burn the more CO2 their mitochondria will make. The more fat they burn the water their mitochondria will make.

When hydrogen is produced from water, the electricity is used to split the water molecule. In chloroplasts sunlight does the job, but in mitochondria the electric plasma in the mitochondrial matirx where the H+ lies does the job.

**How does a hydrogen fuel cell work in a manufactured cell?**
Hydrogen gas is supplied to the anode of the fuel cell. The anode is coated with platinum, which acts as a catalyst to break down the hydrogen into protons and electrons. In cells they use phosphorus or phosphorus containing enzyme catalysts to do the same job.

If a circuit is connected between the anode and cathode then the electrons can travel through the circuit and provide power to any load that is connected as part of the circuit.

Reaction at the anode:

**2H2 → 4H+ + 4e−**

This is an example of an oxidation reaction.

The flow of electrons through the load is the electric current that is generated by the fuel cell.

The hydrogen ions (protons) that are produced from the hydrogen at the anode travel through the electrolyte in the fuel cell to the cathode. Oxygen supplied to the cathode reacts with these hydrogen ions and electrons arriving via the external circuit to produce water and heat, both of which are removed from the fuel cell.

Reaction at the cathode:

**O2 + 4H+ + 4e− → 2H2O**

This is an example of a reduction reaction.

> Jesmond Dalli, Min Zhu, Nikita A. Vlasenko, Bin Deng, Jesper Z. Haeggström, Nicos A. Petasis, and Charles N. Serhan. The novel 13S,14S-epoxy-maresin is converted by human macrophages to maresin 1 (MaR1), inhibits leukotriene A4 hydrolase (LTA4H), and shifts macrophage phenotype. FASEB J July 2013 27:2573-2583; doi:10.1096/fj.13-227728 ;
>
> [http://www.fasebj.org/content/27/7/2573.abstract](https://l.facebook.com/l.php?u=http%3A%2F%2Fwww.fasebj.org%2Fcontent%2F27%2F7%2F2573.abstract&h=ATPFOGh_9AU9-Tx01LHAzta7tbF3TXR0uqYLGGBl_1IM27abVX8GbBxScofqDl3qeYYGw8TkmgNFyCYI3V_EaTa8L7EJFbmibKjlJsKabRn4d1d8O-k5_BJKm53OSGTrGb7sVePdyA&enc=AZOqkkQNBUBr98yt2Me7OuNJpMDcHGZi5y41937VEjvDo5KsMLnYeGZJFHq2V8fMJY8ORna-U9NQFIalWZn_zkEg1nGZ8bzvV44BF2ua_fWUk0V5n6f815UL-UAoWA4-4r4PT-m4-Cle_jnAzxozryL3T2bCwyYzqyTs-0tyUt9XnGWrFwMQmEdLM7yAN3Ze5OK490Pqev4pETbuengTsI-y&s=1)

@[Jack Kruse](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1031/)
When you recommend seafood in the diet, I always think that there must be more to the story. And eventually it will be addressed in latter blogs.

For myself I figured that I should not go much wrong when I get my fatty acids analysis done and adjust my diet accordingly, being mindful to have good amount of DHA.
Is this acceptable approach?

It make sense to me,
but not completely, since it looks like people who are getting sick have very high DHA.
\----------------------------------------------------

In The BodyBio Detoxx Book by Patricia Kane
there is a number of case studies.
Most of those cases present with very high EPA and DHA on beginning of treatment and much lower latter on when the patient is in improved condition.

Case #18 is about Parkinson's (page 212)
It starts with:
EPA =578 %status
DHA=228 %status

Year latter it ends with:
EPA=191 % status
DHA=146 %status
\----------------

%status is calculated relatively to laboratory range.
At the middle of laboratory range %Status=0 (zero)
At the low or high laboratory range % status is (minus 50%) or (plus 50%) respectively.
So at the beginning there was
228/50=4.56 times excess of DHA
and
patient seen relief when her DHA dropped to
146/50=~3 times excess of DHA
so patient still had high excess of DHA, but likely it was somewhat tolerable at that level.

,,

### JanSz — 2017-04-05T16:08:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kinetic-alfven-waves-link-to-plasma-creation-in-mitochondria-h.19303/post-211802>

There are many different types of triglycerides,
with the main division is between saturated and unsaturated types

Triglyceride is an ester derived from glycerol and three fatty acids
membranes contain phospholipids
phospholipids=modified triglycerides
phosphate group replaces one of the fatty acids chains-->that happens on sn3 position
Sn1 position is taken by saturated fatty acids (most of the time)

Sn2 position, position of glycerol where unsaturated FA is added most of time. Exception—surfactant (dipalmityoyl phosphatidylcholine) has saturated FA at both sn1 and sn2 spots.

[https://quizlet.com/1604789/phospholipids-flash-cards/](https://quizlet.com/1604789/phospholipids-flash-cards/)
\-------------------------------------------------------------------------

DHA is unsaturated fatty acids
DHA must go to sn2 position

**Why there is so much emphasis about DHA, that it works properly only when it is in sn2 position?**

\=======================================================================

# Thread: Andrea's journal: Alive
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/andreas-journal-alive.19311/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 32
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Andrea — 2017-04-04T13:10:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/andreas-journal-alive.19311/post-211713>

I can as well start a new journal...

First, a glance back at where I'm coming from:

In the beginning of the previous century, my great grandfather was an electrician who took part in electrifying the city where he lived. When he asked my great grandmother to marry him, she replied "Yes, mr. J., I will marry you - if you promise that our home will be as bright during night as in daytime."
Unfortunately, she got what she asked for.
My grandmother was conceived in this chaos. She was a "difficult" kid with ADHD like behaviour, and all her life she suffered from lung problems (severe asthma/allergy) and continuous skin problems (eczemas and other).

Fast forward to me:
I was a smart kid with high IQ, and I was athletic and strong. In my 20's I was ridiculously fit. However, all my life, every spring and summer I suffered from photodermatosis (skin rashes anywhere on my body when exposed to intense sunlight) and my eyes were sensitive to the sun. I was also allergic to grass, nickel, pollen and some other things.

Not knowing any better, I consumed loads of aluminium and fluoride for years.

At age 29 I moved to a larger city with cell towers on every other rooftop and more wifis than I can count, my lifestyle became more "modern", and within a few years I became fat and fatter, more and more tired, more and more brain dead. Whenever I did something physical I broke: tore a muscle, hurt a joint, got a bruise - always some new injury. I couldn’t even bring home a grocery bag without hurting myself. The docs that I asked for help all told me to eat less and excise more, which is what I did - until I barely ate at all and still kept getting fatter.

In 2011 I was diagnosed with hypothyroidism and was put on synthetic T4 and T3 and cortisone. The docs were shocked at my extremely high LDL and I barely managed to escape statins. Also at that time, they found several anomalies on my ECG and ultrasound, and my cardiologist was seriously worried.

A couple years later I found dr. K.'s site.
I changed a few simple things in my lifestyle. It made a difference, but not good enough. Then I made some less comfortable decisions and changed more...

And now:
\* I haven't taken any meds for more than 28 months.
\* My ECG looks normal.
\* My LDL has dropped to a more normal level.
\* I can wear my old clothes again.
\* My allergies are gone.
\* My red-green colour blindness is gone.
\* I am more at ease with myself and life than ever.
\* Some of my old sports injuries are causing less trouble and less pain.
\* My balance - which was impaired after a concussion in my early 20's - is more or less restored.
\* Last year I didn't have any signs of photodermatosis whatsoever and this winter - after two weeks at 16th or so latitude - I had the best, deepest tan ever.
\* I feel alive!
...
...

So what's next?
I want to keep living. And I want to keep healing my body and brain, my mitochondria; I still have a long way to go - and I am curious about how far I can go... I want to learn to hear my intuition, to listen to it and to follow it. Explore love. Love...

I hope that one day I will get a chance to look you in the eyes and say it to you in person. And I do have a question for you...

### Andrea — 2017-04-19T07:02:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/andreas-journal-alive.19311/post-212554>

I got a surprise recently, when I had my eye sight checked.
When reading letters of smaller and smaller size, they say that 1.0 is full good vision, and the number goes down with age and reduced vision.

When I did my first check, that I remember, at age 17, I had 1.25 on each eye, which they said was exceptional.
In my early 30's, after way too much time in front of computer monitors and out of the sun, I was down at "normal" 1.0/0.9.

Now I'm 40+ and... 1.6. The optician couldn't believe it. They had never encountered anyone who could read those tiny letters during their 35 years in the business - and they didn't have smaller letters for me to read.

I had previously noticed that my vision in the dark has improved remarkably the last few years, but this was very unexpected.

So, I'm older than ever and I can see much better and sharper than ever? Go figure...

What did you have for dinner?

How are you feeling with all the great sunshine? How are you sleeping? Are you a new woman???????

Please document stuff for others ....

### Andrea — 2017-12-07T19:15:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/andreas-journal-alive.19311/post-229734>

I'm getting more relaxed and at ease now. Sleep is quite good, except for some occasional noise and I am sensitive to sounds when I sleep. But I did sleep really well tonight.

One thing I don't really get is that I find my tinnitus more annoying than at home. Even though the frequency of electricity is different and there is less nnEMF. Could it be that the frequency that I hear has changed?

### Jack Kruse — 2017-12-07T19:17:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/andreas-journal-alive.19311/post-229736>

Could be their power grid. One issue in mexico is grounding and they have no codes.

Progresso is pretty rural part of the Yucatan. More time outside away from the grid is wise. It should go. PDC area is way better on that kind of thing. But density is an issue there.

### Andrea — 2017-12-08T00:38:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/andreas-journal-alive.19311/post-229757>

Thanks, dr. K.
Yes, the wiring outside is "interesting" and I can only imagine what they've done inside the buildings. At least my place is at the beach, so I'm not in the midst of it. I have moved my bed to the center of the room, but I don't have my meter with me so I can't be sure it's the best spot...

So, more time on the beach and less on the terrace, which is the only place where I dare to be topless.

### caroline — 2017-12-08T01:54:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/andreas-journal-alive.19311/post-229758>

maybe check with the signal on your phone or laptop? to see where there is less signal in the room.

How was your tour to the cenote?

could you sleep on your terrace?

There are no clothing optional beaches in the Yucatan?????? Could you be topless on the beach? or would there be repercussions?

Do Mexican women wear bikinis?

Are you going to try and stalk Dr. K. in Playa??????? You may trip over him in the sand!

### Andrea — 2017-12-22T22:57:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/andreas-journal-alive.19311/post-230880>

Be alive and wonders shall happen...

The last week has been amazing. And the last three days have been magical. The most wondrous days of my life.

I am very fortunate and grateful.

# Thread: Stomach Function
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stomach-function.19316/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### nonchalant — 2017-04-05T19:15:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stomach-function.19316/post-211818>

A long time ago someone here mentioned that stomach function was a lot more involved than people usually think. I know that water is absorbed through the stomach lining, and of course about the acid and the churning, and the valves at the top and bottom. But I recall that this person talked about the different regions of the stomach doing different things, and I know Jack has mentioned that certain nutrients, such as DHA and D-ribose, are shuttled away quickly, and don't go the usual route. Or is that in the small intestine?

Can someone suggest a good, mid-level book to read? I'm sure it will probably just be conventional wisdom, but I'd like to learn more. Do different branches of the vagus nerve in the stomach function differently?

Which brings up another old forum question from long ago--why does the vagus nerve stop at the descending colon? Inquiring minds want to know!

### drezy — 2017-04-05T19:23:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stomach-function.19316/post-211819>

I'm not aware of any books on this but here are maybe some related articles:

[https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2014/05/140514133438.htm](https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2014/05/140514133438.htm)

[https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3257695/](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3257695/)

Note the ncbi link states something that Jack has to battle regularly:

> Endogenous synthesis of DHA from ALA in humans is much lower and more limited than previously assumed.

### nonchalant — 2017-04-05T20:26:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stomach-function.19316/post-211823>

> [drezy said:](/goto/post?id=211819)
>
> I'm not aware of any books on this but here are maybe some related articles:
>
> [https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2014/05/140514133438.htm](https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2014/05/140514133438.htm)
>
> [https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3257695/](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3257695/)
>
> Note the ncbi link states something that Jack has to battle regularly:

Thanks, Drezy!
Jack doesn't disagree that we can synthesize much DHA, does he? That's why we eat so many oysters. In fact I think recently he mentioned that algae and seaweeds don't help as much, since their DHA isn't paramagnetic, in the SN-2 position. Apparently we aren't that good at flipping the switch. Maybe we need better grounding to be good at it, like our ocean buddies. Or insects, on the other hand.

I know my guinea fowl sure fly a lot after I've fed them some mealworms. On cloudy days they are pretty sedate, but if I feed them worms they will be on the roof and in the trees making all kinds of racket. I guess grass and seeds only go so far. They have cleared out all the grasshoppers in this part of the county (a miracle, I tell ya) so they have to get their DHA somewhere. They have been acting more like chickens, this year, scratching under the leaves for bugs.

### drezy — 2017-04-05T21:08:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stomach-function.19316/post-211828>

@[nonchalant](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/191/)

No mam. The ALA -> DHA conversion efficiency (or lack thereof ) itself is mentioned in that article. AKA don't think your flaxseeds are doing it for you. As a fellow Texan, I assume we are both eating the same gulf oysters which are direct DHA.

I assume you have the guinea fowl for the eggs. I always had a minor carnivore's moral dilemma until I tried raising chickens. On a perfect 3 acre setup they one-by-one chose to run to the neighbor's fence, hop it, and get crushed and gnawed on by a dog. Though I have a low tolerance for animal cruelty, I realized that mankind has domesticated these creatures into something incredibly dumb. I still feel a moral weight with being a carnivore, but not so much with chickens anymore.

# Thread: What it takes to be a leader
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-it-takes-to-be-a-leader.19318/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### drezy — 2017-04-05T22:40:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-it-takes-to-be-a-leader.19318/post-211835>

"What it takes to be a leader" often sounds daunting, important, and complex.

I'd love to be a leader of my own mother's exploration of the mitochondrial approach here. I'd personally lop off a few fingers if that could be the case. I'm also trying to be the leader of my son who is experiencing the awkward 12 yo world. I'd lop off more appendages if I could just get him to understand that lots of people just don't matter and that you have to stay focused on your goal. If I got these two wishes I'd be pretty appendage-less.

I've worked into leadership positions and fallen into them. All of us who have experienced an improvement of condition are in a leadership position, like it or not. Once you "arrived" there is no solution, salve, or quick fix. There is no fan-base, no cheering crowds, and no blatantly clear victories.

I don't have the answers to how you lead the changes you want, but I found historical context to be useful.

@[Jack Kruse](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1031/) has to sometimes tell people to not look at [the finger that's pointing at the moon](https://essenceofbuddhism.wordpress.com/2016/04/19/what-the-finger-pointing-to-the-moon-analogy-really-means-from-zen-buddhism-the-buddha-in-the-shurangama-sutra/). I think leading any kid of mentality change,which we all seem to want, involves that. There is a kitsch and sometimes shallow messaging in the west around images or statues of Buddha. I guess it's supposed to evoke some idea or another.

The Sekhiyas in buddhist monastery text illuminate what may have to be involved in leadership. These were "rules of conduct" established by Buddha himself for his monastery. From the most enlightened being and his devout followers sprung this divine rule: [don't chew like a cow](http://en.dhammadana.org/sangha/vinaya/227/75sk2.htm#ch----50). Straight from the Buddha there. I had to pass up the "Don't shake food particles on fellow diners" and "don't dribble stuff out of your lips". My point is here is this enlightened being arriving to a monastery after a journey that might kill you or me and establishing extremely basic eating etiquette end up being a task the "enlightened one" just has to tackle up front. It sounds kinda Marine commander level, but I think that may be floor 1 of where leadership starts.

My only point in writing this is that I suspect that, if you want to lead change, you may have togged down to the Marine commander/Buddha level of non-antiseptic human interaction.

### Jack Kruse — 2017-04-06T01:08:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-it-takes-to-be-a-leader.19318/post-211848>

Leaders speak a language all their own. While the words used might be in your language, or any other commonly used language, Leader speak delivers a different message than when similar thoughts and sentences are conveyed by others. Words are like nets; we hope they'll cover what we mean, but we know they can't possibly hold that much joy, or grief, or wonder. Leaders measure their words to make maximum impact to the audience they are speaking to that particular day.

### drezy — 2017-04-06T02:11:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-it-takes-to-be-a-leader.19318/post-211849>

Just sayin... A guy like the Buddha had to descend to the level of asking people to not chew with their mouths open or flick food particles on others.

Every time I've successfully lead others, way more menial hand holding was involved. Whenever I failed as a leader is when I lost touch with the menial (and often seemingly demeaning) aspects of what the task entailed.

Your policy of not wasting energy on people who don't really want to hear natural wisdom related to the sun makes total sense to me.

I am related to people that are highly valuable to me that don't necessarily want to hear this all yet. I've made peace with the possibility that they just never will. This thread is about acquiring and condensing the leadership required to start to change those most beloved but most stubborn minds of your members. Again, it may be completely futile and I'd be happy to become a Sisyphus for the people I most care about.

Am I possibly the one putting my mask on first in a malfunctioning (cultural) plane wondering if things could have been different? Highly likely. I wasn't looking at all into any of this and stumbled like a big idiot into this whole perspective. My benefits are obvious to me and to some of those around me that are somewhat observant.

Still I feel unaccomplished in leading this change for some of the people in my life who could most use this knowledge. My little inter&post generational family get it and sun & CT with me. I'd love, however, to reach down and cinch up my cahones to get my mother/mitochodrial donor to follow suit. That's really the tender/painful reason I started this thread.

Getting your own oxygen mask on is great. What next? How best to influence?

I would like to better lead those in my life.

# Thread: Leaky gut , zapping, black seed oil
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leaky-gut-zapping-black-seed-oil.19321/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### TANN — 2017-04-07T18:03:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leaky-gut-zapping-black-seed-oil.19321/post-211963>

Hello all

My issues are as follows; leaky gut, adrenal health, brain fog, psoriasis, constipation, anxiety, histamine intolerance, difficulty losing weight (currently 15 st 4lb)
Ive had the gut issues for around 15 years but possibly all my life to a lesser extent. Ive made massive progress from where i had palpitations and black outs at my worst. These days i have the occasional skin flare up i feel tired after evening meal and caffeine has been a crutch for a long time. I know i still have a long way to go. I have recently been restoring stomach acid by taking HCL betain/pepsin and have got my BM much better than normal. I am doing low carb high fat and high protein but if i try less than 50g i get really constipated and the skin issues come back. I make my own sauerkraut and i use live apple cider vinegar with each meal.
I find mediation fantastic and do 5 mins pranayama breathing following by 10 minutes deep (mantra) meditation per day.
Recently been on a protocol including L glutamine, NAG, NAC, Ester C, Q10 probiotics (have done this many times before also) I also take Mg theronate at night which helps big time with achy joints and sleep.
With Jacks recommendations I also now make sure im getting more daylight im eating fresh sardines twice per week and i use grounding mat in the car. plus i do sauna/cold showers three times a week.
Recently im using my terminator 2 zapper directly on gut and i have seen very positive results; i have better skin , my energy has improved and less anxiety. Last night i slept for 10 hours when i typically get just 6-7 . I wanted to know others experience of zapping and of course Jacks opinion. (got a horrible feeling he wont condone it!)
Finally i would like to know opinions of black seed oil as well, i believe Jack doesn't recommend it but would like to know more please. It helps me lose weight i can make me a little aggressive as it builds testosterone. I know that it has omega 6 and not 3 which wouldn't be good but i wanted to know is it really that bad?
Thats my introduction and questions for now.
Best of luck to everyone

### Sue-UK — 2017-04-08T13:44:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leaky-gut-zapping-black-seed-oil.19321/post-211995>

Hi TANN

"have done this many times before also" on that supplement protocol speaks volumes. Your post mentions lots of supplements and a zapper, and it seems as if you are only just dipping a toe into the water as far as the teaching here goes? Getting more daylight is good .... but how much and when is important, as is where you live (population density, pollution etc). How are you following the circadian light cycle? Eating sardines twice a week is good, how are you planning to improve on that?

Have you read [https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/your-first-post-to-read-here-at-jackkruse-com.17829/](https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/your-first-post-to-read-here-at-jackkruse-com.17829/).

Welcome to the forum.

### Remo — 2017-04-30T14:36:34+0300

Hey man, welcome to the forums! im having similar complaints as yours and I can tell you that your on the right track. Sleep is crucial, sunlight essential, clean water necessary and high intensity training needed. Taking it step by step is the key imo.

**Static Back**

1.  Lie on your **back** with your legs up over your inflatable large block or up over a chair/couch.
2.  Your arms are at 45 degrees and your palms up.
3.  Try to relax your upper **back** and notice if your low **back** is flat evenly from left to right.
4.  HOLD.

I have been doing CT for the past month and started with short cold showers increasing length
of time. I have upgraded to immersion in a tub with frozen Ice bricks and at the moment I am able to
stay in the tub for 20 mins and hope to build on this.

I have noticed my energy levels have increased and better clarity of thought with much deeper sleep
but I wasn't expecting old scar tissue to heal as it has. Scars have become smaller and have blended in
with surrounding skin much to my surprise. I actually look forward to CT which I have been doing each evening.

Friends have noticed on the complexion of my skin (glow) of late. Getting several hours of morning Sun
with Grounding and increased DHA from wild Salmon most days ,,feeling great !
Please any one with thoughts on skin health and CT I would welcome your input.

### TANN — 2017-04-09T12:49:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fungal-issues-leptin-and-ct.19325/post-212047>

I'm considering diving into the leptin reset again whilst trying the CT (face)
I have long standing gut issues. Usually when I eat less carbs I start to get backed up with sluggish digestion.
My question is this, can I expect the leptin reset and CT along with plenty of daylight to correct my gut issues. I have tried many times to sort out my candida baceteria overload but feel that I have some very resistant fungals deep in my gut wall.
Would live to know opinions on this if anyone wants to share. Many thanks

### Allin — 2017-04-13T21:13:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fungal-issues-leptin-and-ct.19325/post-212280>

When your Redox is good, you will not have gut issues. Fixing gut issues starts with fixing your eye Clock first, then receptors on your skin, and then gut and lungs are next. Face dawn sun for 10-15 minutes as naked as you can get. Then AM sun as naked as you can get with some UV (I use D-minder application which shows when UV is available for your location). Start with 10-15 minutes each day to build up a sun callous. Drink bottled, well or spring water with NO fluoride, chlorine or bromide in it. Start off eating 3 raw oysters twice a week and increase that to 6 after 3-4 weeks. Start doing face dunks after two weeks of SUN. After two weeks of face dunks, start with cold water in tub for 30 minutes for another two weeks. If you can do the 30 minutes of tub and then the AM sun with UV in it would be best, but if not then do the 30 minutes of tub in the evening. Start adding ice after two weeks to the tub.

So after a month: you should have your dawn sun for 15 minutes, ice baths 30-45 minutes, UV sun for 30-60 minutes, 6 raw oysters twice a week, drinking good water every day. Protect your eyes in the evening, after 8 PM by wearing some type of blue blocking glasses. My rule of thumb, if you have had gut issues for 6 years; then I target & reassess 6 months out. I know you can do it and you will be amazed at how you feel six months from now.

> My garden is great as it gets sun most of the day, except unfortunately the first hour or so. That means I'll have to take a walk I suppose. Would you recommend walking into the sunlight then as soon as dawn comes. How critical is all this anyway does it matter if I'm 30 minutes late. Sorry if I sound negative. And thanks again for you help

_Farmed raised oysters are fine. And no I am not a fan of raw oysters either but a little hot sauce and I chug them. Being late for the sun at dawn is ok, just get it done within an hour of sunrise, but shoot for dawn. Just change a little every day you are worth it. I try to keep the actions simple and the cost as close to free as possible and the sun is free._

# Thread: Carb question on Leptin RX
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/carb-question-on-leptin-rx.19326/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### shaneoco — 2017-04-10T10:37:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/carb-question-on-leptin-rx.19326/post-212097>

Hi, I've been doing the Leptin RX for about 2 weeks and am wondering about the amount of carbs I can eat. I am very cautious with this as I burned out with adrenal fatigue before with a very low carb/ keto diet with too much intermittant fasting (skipping breakfast!) and lots of exercise. Right now I'm eating a high-fat, high-protein diet with approx 150-200g of carbs a day. I find that if I lower my carb consumption I get a real stressy feeling (craving) even though I amn't actually hungry . If I eat a maybe 50-70g carbs it's then gone. So I'm guessing that even though I'm eating large amounts of fat (50g or more of butter or coconut oil) and protein per meal my body isn't really using it as fuel and when the carbs run low I'm getting the stressy/ craving feeling telling my body to eat more carbs again. Is this correct or am I missing something?

I'm 5'8, 65kg, lean but muscular build. Follow a fairly strict Epi-paleo diet and exercise minimally most days. Keep a good eye on my light situation, especially after sundown. I definitely don't want to lose
weight and could probably handle another 2-5kgs comfortably.
I'd be very slow to try keto/ v low carb again just because of the last time as I'm still not over the adrenal issue - have very poor fragmented sleep/ salt cravings etc. On a side note - sleep has been majorly improved by just doing a week of CT.

1) So I'm wondering is it a waste of time doing the Leptin RX and CT with so many carbs per day or will it just take longer? Please keep in mind I'm in the Northern hemisphere with 16 hours of daylight now.
2) And, why does Jack recommend having most of your daily carb intake in the morning and least in the evening? This seems counter-intuitive to other adrenal protocols which advocate a higher carb meal in the evening to help with sleep.
3) If all the fat I'm eating clearly isn't getting burned as fuel, what is happening to it?

Would appreciate if anyone could shed any light on this. Thank you.
